title: OLAP Columnar Warehouse: NFR Specification Required
description: Meta-policy pattern that activates when olap_workload is enabled and enforces that OLAP-specific NFR fields are explicitly specified. OLAP engines differ fundamentally from OLTP databases: columnar storage optimises aggregations over wide scans, query latency is seconds rather than milliseconds, write throughput is ingestion-batch rather than high-frequency point writes, and concurrency limits are orders of magnitude lower. Without explicit peak_query_per_second_read and p95Milliseconds, the compiler cannot select the correct warehouse implementation: a 50ms p95 target implies ClickHouse or an acceleration layer, while a 2-second p95 is fine for BigQuery or Redshift. This pattern also enforces that read-your-writes consistency is not expected, since warehouse data lags the OLTP source by at least one pipeline cycle.

cost.costNotes: Meta-policy pattern with no direct cost. Warehouse infrastructure costs are in the variant patterns (data-olap-warehouse--aws, --gcp, --azure, --cloud-agnostic, --self-hosted).

provenance.estimatedMonthlyRangeUsd: Zero cost — meta-policy pattern only. Compute and storage costs are in downstream variant patterns.
provenance.adoptionCost: Zero adoption cost — this pattern auto-activates when olap_workload: true is set and enforces NFR specification as a hard compilation requirement.
provenance.licenseCost: No license cost — policy enforcement pattern only.

provides.reasoning: Enforces that OLAP-specific NFR fields (peak_query_per_second_read, p95Milliseconds) are explicitly declared when olap_workload is enabled, preventing under-specified analytical architectures.

availability.reason: General architectural enforcement pattern applicable to any OLAP workload regardless of technology or cloud provider

supports_nfr.reason: OLAP warehouses are compatible with any auth mechanism; warehouse access is controlled via IAM roles, service accounts, or SQL-level user permissions.
All major managed warehouses provide query audit logs (CloudTrail for Redshift, BigQuery audit logs, Snowflake ACCOUNT_USAGE). Compatible with audited and non-audited environments.
OLAP warehouses support tenant isolation through schema-per-tenant, row-level security, or dedicated warehouse clusters per tenant.
OLAP warehouses support PII workloads with column-level encryption, dynamic data masking (Snowflake, BigQuery), and row-level security policies.
Pattern is GDPR-agnostic; compliance depends on data handling in the application layer, not this pattern.
Pattern is CCPA-agnostic; compliance depends on data handling in the application layer, not this pattern.
Pattern is HIPAA-agnostic; compliance depends on data handling and provider BAAs, not this pattern.
Pattern is SOX-agnostic; compliance depends on audit controls in the application layer, not this pattern.

supports_constraints.reason: Primary activation gate: this pattern only applies when olap_workload is explicitly enabled.
OLAP warehouse gating pattern is cloud-agnostic; variant selection handles cloud-specific implementation.
Warehouse interaction is primarily via SQL. All major languages have client libraries or JDBC/ODBC drivers.
OLAP gating pattern is platform-agnostic; any system enabling olap_workload must specify OLAP NFRs regardless of primary platform type.
Mixed OLTP/OLAP architectures are common; the warehouse is the read-side complement to an OLTP store.
OLAP warehouses are commonly loaded via batch ETL but also support streaming ingestion.
Streaming ingestion into a warehouse (Firehose, Datastream, Snowpipe) is supported but adds pipeline complexity.

warn_nfr.reason: Minimum of 1 analytical query per second. Below this, a general-purpose RDBMS or even DuckDB on a single machine is sufficient without dedicated warehouse infrastructure.
warn_nfr.message: data-olap-warehouse: peak read QPS is {actual} (<1/s). At this query rate a dedicated OLAP warehouse adds cost and operational overhead without benefit — consider a general-purpose RDBMS with appropriate indexes, or DuckDB for local/embedded analytics.

warn_nfr.reason: OLAP warehouses reflect source data after pipeline completion. Read-your-writes consistency is not guaranteed — queries may not see data written in the last pipeline cycle.
warn_nfr.message: data-olap-warehouse: spec requires needsReadYourWrites but OLAP warehouses cannot satisfy this. Data lags the OLTP source by at least one pipeline cycle (typically minutes to hours). Serve read-your-writes queries from the OLTP source database; use the warehouse for analytical queries only.

warn_nfr.reason: No columnar warehouse engine can reliably serve complex analytical queries at sub-50ms p95. Below this threshold, a pre-aggregated cache, materialized view layer, or purpose-built in-memory OLAP engine is required instead.
warn_nfr.message: data-olap-warehouse: p95 latency target is {actual}ms (<50ms). No general-purpose OLAP warehouse achieves sub-50ms p95 for ad-hoc analytical queries. Options: (1) ClickHouse with materialised views pre-computing the exact aggregation; (2) a caching layer (Redis, Druid) in front of the warehouse; (3) pre-aggregated summary tables served by a low-latency store.
